skill.name: ai-avatar-video
skill.description: Create AI avatar, talking head, lipsync, dubbing, and UGC-style videos through the inference.sh belt CLI.
skill.summary: Generate avatar videos from portraits, scripts, or audio using inference.sh models.
skill.author: qu-skills

security_audit.summary: Static external command findings are mostly Markdown command examples for the intended belt CLI workflow, not hidden executable code. The weak cryptography finding at SKILL.md line 3 is a false positive, but the skill still carries medium risk because it authorizes networked CLI calls that upload images, audio, scripts, and video URLs to third-party inference services.

medium_findings.title: Networked CLI Execution for Remote Inference
medium_findings.description: The skill allows Bash(belt *) and instructs users to run belt app commands. This is expected for the skill, but it can execute remote inference jobs and should be reviewed before publication.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools declaration and multiple belt app examples clearly show intended external CLI execution. The commands are not hidden or obfuscated, so the risk is operational rather than confirmed malicious.

medium_findings.title: User Media and Scripts Sent to Third-Party Services
medium_findings.description: The workflows use external image, audio, and video URLs and send scripts to inference.sh model providers. This may expose personal likenesses, voice content, or commercial scripts to third-party processing.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file repeatedly documents remote URLs and remote model execution for avatar, TTS, transcription, and lipsync workflows. No credential theft is evident, but sensitive media transfer is inherent to the workflow.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Finding Is a False Positive
low_findings.description: The high static finding at SKILL.md line 3 does not correspond to cryptographic code or hash usage. It appears to match descriptive text in front matter, with no evidence of weak cryptographic behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 is the description metadata for avatar video capabilities. I found no cryptographic function, algorithm selection, or security control on that line.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Flagged as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: Many external command findings are caused by fenced bash examples and inline commands in documentation. They are not hidden scripts, but they still teach users to run the belt CLI.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The static locations align with Markdown code spans and code fences. The content is visible documentation rather than executable code embedded in the skill package.

dangerous_patterns.title: Permissive belt CLI Tool Access
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill grants Bash access for any belt command. This can trigger network calls, account actions, or model jobs depending on user credentials and belt configuration.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools front matter explicitly permits Bash(belt *). This is a broad external command permission even though it is aligned with the skill purpose.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Media URL Processing
dangerous_patterns.description: The examples pass image, audio, and video URLs to remote model runs. Users should avoid private media unless they accept third-party processing.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Remote URL fields appear throughout the documented workflows. The security impact depends on the sensitivity of the media submitted.

content.value_statement: Avatar video workflows often require several model tools and media steps. This skill provides guided belt CLI commands for talking head, TTS, lipsync, and dubbing workflows.
actual_capabilities: Shows belt CLI commands for P-Video-Avatar, OmniHuman, Fabric, and PixVerse Lipsync.
Guides text-to-avatar workflows with built-in TTS voices and language options.
Documents portrait generation followed by avatar video creation.
Outlines dubbing workflows with transcription, translation, TTS, and lipsync steps.
Provides batch UGC examples for multiple presenter voices.
Links to related inference.sh documentation and companion skills.
limitations: Requires the inference.sh belt CLI and a configured account.
Remote model runs may incur costs and processing delays.
Inputs may include personal images, voices, scripts, or private video URLs.
Quality depends on source media, model availability, and provider limits.

use_cases.title: Marketing Avatar Ads
use_cases.description: Create UGC-style presenter videos from product scripts and portrait images.

use_cases.title: Localized Training Clips
use_cases.description: Generate translated speech and lipsync videos for internal training content.

use_cases.title: Character Dialogue Videos
use_cases.description: Produce avatar speech clips for game characters, NPCs, or virtual presenters.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Simple Avatar Video
prompt_templates.prompt: Use ai-avatar-video to create a talking head video from my portrait and this short script. Recommend the simplest model and explain required inputs.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a UGC Campaign Batch
prompt_templates.prompt: Use ai-avatar-video to plan three UGC avatar videos for one product. Vary presenter voice, tone, and visual direction.

prompt_templates.title: Localize an Existing Video
prompt_templates.prompt: Use ai-avatar-video to outline a workflow for dubbing my video into another language with transcription, TTS, and lipsync.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Avatar Models
prompt_templates.prompt: Use ai-avatar-video to compare P-Video-Avatar, OmniHuman, Fabric, and PixVerse for my project goals, cost, speed, and quality needs.

output_examples.input: A marketer needs a short product demo with a friendly AI presenter.
output_examples.output: A recommended P-Video-Avatar workflow, suggested portrait direction, voice choice, script notes, and expected resolution settings.

output_examples.input: A course team needs the same lesson intro in another language.
output_examples.output: A step-by-step dubbing plan that covers transcription, translated narration, speech generation, and lipsync model selection.

output_examples.input: A game designer wants NPC dialogue clips from character portraits.
output_examples.output: A model recommendation, voice style guidance, audio requirements, and notes about using clear portraits for better facial animation.

best_practices: Use clear, front-facing portraits with good lighting and permission from the subject.
Review provider terms before submitting private images, voices, scripts, or customer media.
Start with P-Video-Avatar for simple workflows before using multi-step model chains.
anti_patterns: Do not upload private likenesses or voices without consent.
Do not run batch jobs without checking model cost and account limits.
Do not use low-quality source audio when lipsync accuracy is important.

faq.question: What does this skill help create?
faq.answer: It helps create AI avatar, talking head, lipsync, dubbing, and UGC-style videos through belt CLI workflows.

faq.question: Does it generate videos directly inside Claude or Codex?
faq.answer: No. It provides commands and workflow guidance for external inference.sh model runs.

faq.question: Which model should I try first?
faq.answer: The skill recommends P-Video-Avatar first because it includes TTS and supports simple portrait-to-video workflows.

faq.question: Can it use my own audio file?
faq.answer: Yes. Several documented workflows accept audio URLs for driving avatar motion or lipsync.

faq.question: What privacy risks should I consider?
faq.answer: Avatar workflows may send images, voices, scripts, and video URLs to third-party services for processing.

faq.question: Can it help with localization?
faq.answer: Yes. It outlines workflows for transcription, translated speech generation, and lipsync in another language.
